subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves, thirds, or fourths and describe these parts and the whole.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into equal squares by making rows and columns and count how many squares there are.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and draw shapes having specified attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces. (Sizes are compared directly or visually, not compared by measuring.) Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and draw shapes by their features, like number of sides or equal faces, and name shapes like triangles and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child understands shapes and their features and can think about how they are alike and different.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph.
description_plain: Your child can make picture and bar graphs with up to four categories and use them to solve simple problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.
description_plain: Your child can measure several objects, record the lengths on a line plot, and organize the data.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies, using $ and ¢ symbols appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve money problems using coins and dollar bills and write amounts with $ and ¢ symbols.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0,1,2,…, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show numbers on a number line and use it to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as drawings of rulers) and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about lengths using addition and subtraction within 100, with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
description_plain: Your child understands how addition and subtraction relate to measuring length.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate lengths using inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure something twice using different units and explain how the measurements change with the unit size.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long things are using tools like rulers or measuring tapes.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate lengths using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Data, Statistics, and Probability
description_plain: Your child learns about data, statistics, and probability.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find out how many objects are in a rectangle with up to 5 rows and 5 columns, and can write an equation to show this.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members, e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2s; write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing or counting by twos and write an equation showing even numbers as two equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
description_plain: Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies. (See 1.OA.C.6 for a list of strategies.) By end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 20 in their head and remember all sums of one-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve one- and two-step word problems using addition and subtraction within 100, drawing pictures and writing equations with unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work, using place value and the properties of operations. (Explanations may be supported by drawings or objects.)
description_plain: Your child can explain why their ways of adding and subtracting work by using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100–900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100–900.
description_plain: Your child can add or subtract 10 or 100 in their head to numbers between 100 and 900.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 1000, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method. Understand that in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, one adds or subtracts hundreds and hundreds, tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose or decompose tens or hundreds.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using drawings or models and understand how to add hundreds, tens, and ones separately.

subject: Math
description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math properties.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 100 using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones, and use the symbols >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in different ways, like with digits, words, or expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, etc., mean one, two, three, and more hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens — called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is made of ten groups of ten.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that a three-digit number shows how many hundreds, tens, and ones it has, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the structure of problems to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools in smart ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking and listen to others to understand math better.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and ideas in different ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child tries to understand problems and keeps working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters, and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares. Understand for these examples that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves and quarters to describe them.

subject: Math
description: Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, and quarter-circles) or three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape. (Students do not need to learn formal names, such as "right rectangular prisms.")
description_plain: Your child can put together flat shapes or solid shapes to make new shapes and can also break apart shapes to make others.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size); build and draw shapes to possess defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell the important features that define shapes, like a triangle having three sides, and can build and draw shapes with those features.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can collect and organize data into up to three groups and ask and answer questions about how many are in each group and how the groups compare.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps. Limit to contexts where the object being measured is spanned by a whole number of length units with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller, same-sized objects end to end without gaps or overlaps.

subject: Math
description: Order three objects by length; compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.
description_plain: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare two objects by using a third object.

subject: Math
description: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths by using smaller units repeatedly or by measuring indirectly.

subject: Math
description: Data, Statistics, and Probability
description_plain: Your child learns about data, statistics, and probability.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the missing number in an addition or subtraction problem with three numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign, and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
description_plain: Your child understands what the equal sign means and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
description_plain: Your child can work with addition and subtraction equations.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction within 10. Use strategies such as counting on; making ten (e.g., 8+6=8+2+4=10+4=14); decomposing a number leading to a ten (e.g., 13−4=13−3−1=10−1=9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g., knowing that 8+4=12, one knows 12−8=4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6+7 by creating the known equivalent 6+6+1=12+1=13).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 and quickly add and subtract numbers up to 10 using helpful tricks like making ten or breaking numbers apart.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by counting on 2 to add 2).
description_plain: Your child can use counting to help add and subtract numbers, like counting on to add.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve subtraction problems by figuring out what number needs to be added to reach a total.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract. (Students need not use formal terms for these properties.)
description_plain: Your child uses simple rules about adding and subtracting to help solve problems, even if they don’t know the formal names.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child understands and uses the rules for adding and subtracting, and knows how they are connected.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three numbers with a total of 20 or less using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with addition and subtraction within 20 by using objects, drawings, or simple equations with a blank for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can show and solve problems using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 from multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 (positive or zero differences), using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from each other using objects or drawings and explain how they solved it.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than a two-digit number in their head and explain how they know.

subject: Math
description: Add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used. Understand that in adding two-digit numbers, one adds tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding two-digit and one-digit numbers or multiples of 10, using objects or drawings and explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of tens and ones to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at the tens and ones, and use symbols like >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, etc., mean groups of tens and zero ones.

subject: Math
description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some ones.

subject: Math
description: 10 can be thought of as a bundle of ten ones — called a "ten."
description_plain: Your child understands that 10 ones can be grouped together to make one 'ten.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that a two-digit number shows how many tens and how many ones it has.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120, starting at any number less than 120. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count, read, and write numbers up to 120, starting from any number less than 120, and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the structure of problems to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools in smart ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking and listen to others to understand math better.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and ideas in different ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child tries to understand problems and keeps working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes. For example, "Can you join these two triangles with full sides touching to make a rectangle?"
description_plain: Your child can put simple shapes together to make bigger shapes, like joining two triangles to make a rectangle.

subject: Math
description: Model shapes in the world by building shapes from components (e.g., sticks and clay balls) and drawing shapes.
description_plain: Your child can build and draw shapes they see around them using parts like sticks and clay balls.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/"corners") and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).
description_plain: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and talk about how they are alike and different, including counting sides and corners.

subject: Math
description: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
description_plain: Your child can explore, compare, create, and put shapes together.

subject: Math
description: Identify shapes as two-dimensional (lying in a plane, "flat") or three-dimensional ("solid").
description_plain: Your child can tell if a shape is flat (2D) or solid (3D).

subject: Math
description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientations or overall size.
description_plain: Your child can correctly name shapes no matter how they are turned or how big they are.

subject: Math
description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of these objects using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
description_plain: Your child can name shapes they see around them and describe where things are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
description_plain: Your child can identify and talk about shapes like squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count. (Limit category counts to be less than or equal to 10.)
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group (up to 10), and put the groups in order by size.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects and count the number of objects in each category.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups and count how many are in each group.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which object has "more of"/"less of" the attribute, and describe the difference. For example, directly compare the heights of two children and describe one child as taller/shorter.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects to see which one is longer, heavier, or has more of something and describe the difference.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of objects, such as length or weight. Describe several measurable attributes of a single object. (CCSS: K.MD.A.1)
description_plain: Your child can describe things you can measure about objects, like how long or heavy they are, and describe several of these for one object.

subject: Math
description: Describe and compare measurable attributes.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare things that can be measured.

subject: Math
description: Data, Statistics, and Probability
description_plain: Your child learns about data, statistics, and probability.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record the answer with a drawing or equation.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number to make 10, using objects or drawings, and write the answer.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 5=2+3 and 5=4+1).
description_plain: Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in different ways using objects or drawings and show each way with a drawing or equation.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, e.g., by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems and add or subtract numbers up to 10 using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, drawings (drawings need not show details, but should show the mathematics in the problem), sounds (e.g., claps), acting out situations, verbal explanations, expressions, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, drawings, sounds, acting, or words.

subject: Math
description: Model and describe addition as putting together and adding to, and subtraction as taking apart and taking from, using objects or drawings.
description_plain: Your child understands addition as putting things together or adding more, and subtraction as taking things apart or away, using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some further ones, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each composition or decomposition by a drawing or equation (such as 18=10+8); understand that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some extra ones using objects or drawings and write it down.

subject: Math
description: Work with numbers 11–19 to gain foundations for place value.
description_plain: Your child works with numbers 11 to 19 to learn about tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers from 1 to 10 written as numerals to see which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, e.g., by using matching and counting strategies. (Include groups with up to 10 objects.)
description_plain: Your child can tell if one group of up to 10 objects has more, less, or the same number as another group by matching or counting.

subject: Math
description: Count to answer "how many?" questions about as many as 20 things arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 things in a scattered configuration; given a number from 1–20, count out that many objects.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 20 objects arranged in a line, rectangle, or circle, and up to 10 objects scattered, and can count out a given number of objects up to 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.
description_plain: Your child understands that when you say the next number in counting, it means one more than the number before.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.
description_plain: Your child knows that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object.
description_plain: Your child can count objects by saying number names in order, matching one number to each object.

subject: Math
description: Apply the relationship between numbers and quantities and connect counting to cardinality.
description_plain: Your child connects counting numbers to the amount of objects counted.

subject: Math
description: Count to determine the number of objects.
description_plain: Your child can count objects to find out how many there are.

subject: Math
description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0–20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and match written numbers to groups of objects, including zero for no objects.

subject: Math
description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).
description_plain: Your child can count forward starting from any number they know, not just from 1.

subject: Math
description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
description_plain: Your child can count to 100 by ones and by tens.

subject: Math
description: Use number names and the count sequence.
description_plain: Your child uses number names and counts in order.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the structure of problems to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools in smart ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking and listen to others to understand math better.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and ideas in different ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child tries to understand problems and keeps working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume, and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems about the area, volume, and surface area of shapes like triangles, rectangles, cubes, and prisms.

subject: Math
description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multistep problem to write and solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use facts about different types of angles to write and solve equations to find unknown angles in shapes.

subject: Math
description: State the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and use them to solve problems; give an informal derivation of the relationship between the circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and can use them to solve problems, and understands how circumference and area are related.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems about angles, area, surface area, and volume.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in cross sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shapes you get when you slice solid shapes like rectangular prisms and pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes by hand, with tools, or on a computer using given measurements, and can tell when these measurements make one, more than one, or no possible triangles.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings by finding real lengths and areas from the drawing and making new scale drawings at different sizes.

subject: Math
description: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
description_plain: Your child can draw and build shapes and explain how they relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables, and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined events using organized lists, tables, or tree diagrams, and pick out the outcomes that match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Explain that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events happening is the fraction of all possible outcomes where those events occur.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the chances of combined events happening by using lists, tables, tree diagrams, or simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child creates a probability model based on how often things happen in real-life chance events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child makes a simple probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and uses it to find chances of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child builds probability models to find chances of events and compares these to actual results, explaining differences.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child estimates the chance of an event by collecting data and watching how often it happens over time.

subject: Math
description: Explain that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of something happening is a number between 0 and 1, where bigger numbers mean it’s more likely to happen.

subject: Math
description: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
description_plain: Your child explores chance events and learns how to create, use, and check probability models.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child uses averages and variation from random samples to compare two groups and make guesses about them.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability.
description_plain: Your child looks at two sets of data to see how much they overlap and compares their centers using measures of spread.

subject: Math
description: Draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child makes informal comparisons between two groups using data.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child uses data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and tries multiple samples to see how results can change.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; explain that generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Explain that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a small, random part of a group can give good information about the whole group if the sample is fair.

subject: Math
description: Use random sampling to draw inferences about a population.
description_plain: Your child uses random samples to learn about larger groups.

subject: Math
description: Data, Statistics, and Probability
description_plain: Your child learns about data, statistics, and probability.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px±q > r, px±q ≥ r, px±q < r, or px±q ≤ r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that use inequalities like greater than or less than, graph the answers, and explain what the graph means for the problem.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px ± q = r and p(x±q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently. Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with equations like px ± q = r or p(x ± q) = r, solve them easily, and compare algebra and arithmetic methods.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers in real problems and write simple equations or inequalities to solve them.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems with positive and negative numbers in different forms, use math tools wisely, and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using number and algebra expressions and equations.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
description_plain: Your child understands that changing how an expression looks can help explain the problem and how the numbers relate.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to add, subtract, factor, and expand expressions with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child uses math properties to create expressions that mean the same thing.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division; know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction or rational number into a decimal by dividing and knows decimals either end or repeat.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to multiply and divide rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then -(p/q) = -p/q = p/-q. Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands dividing integers works when the divisor isn't zero and explains division of rational numbers using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (−1)(−1)=1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying rational numbers follows math rules, including multiplying negatives, and explains these products with real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division and of fractions to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide positive and negative numbers, including fractions, and use these skills to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about adding and subtracting numbers to help solve problems with fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p-q = p+(-q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands subtraction of rational numbers as adding the opposite and can find the distance between two numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate p+q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands adding numbers means moving left or right on a number line depending on positive or negative values and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0.
description_plain: Your child can explain situations where two opposite amounts combine to make zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract rational numbers; represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers and show these on a number line going left-right or up-down.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers using what they know about fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve multistep ratio and percent problems.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and proportions to solve multi-step problems involving percentages and ratios.

subject: Math
description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0,0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph mean in a proportional relationship, especially the starting point and the unit rate.

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships by equations.
description_plain: Your child can write equations that show proportional relationships between quantities.

subject: Math
description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the unit rate (constant ratio) in tables, graphs, equations, pictures, and words.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship, e.g., by testing for equivalent ratios in a table or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.
description_plain: Your child can tell if two quantities have a proportional relationship by checking if their ratios are equal or if their graph is a straight line through zero.

subject: Math
description: Identify and represent proportional relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can find and show proportional relationships between two quantities.

subject: Math
description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas, and other quantities measured in like or different units.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates from ratios, including those with fractions and different units like length or area.

subject: Math
description: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use ratios to solve problems in everyday life and math.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the structure of problems to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools in smart ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking and listen to others to understand math better.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and ideas in different ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child tries to understand problems and keeps working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: State the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and can use them to solve real and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving the volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both 2D and 3D problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can explain facts about angles in triangles and parallel lines and how to tell if triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations; given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.
description_plain: Your child can show that two shapes are similar if one can be changed into the other by rotating, flipping, sliding, or resizing, and describe how to do it.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effect of dilations, translations, rotations, and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can describe how moving, flipping, turning, or resizing shapes changes their position using coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations; given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child can show that two shapes are congruent if one can be changed into the other by rotating, flipping, or sliding, and explain the steps.

subject: Math
description: Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child understands that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Angles are taken to angles of the same measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles keep their size after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.
description_plain: Your child understands that lines and line segments keep their length after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations:
description_plain: Your child can test and confirm how rotations, reflections, and slides change shapes.

subject: Math
description: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
description_plain: Your child uses models or tools to understand when shapes are the same size and shape or just similar.

subject: Math
description: Explain that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table. Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects. Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can make and read two-way tables to see how two categories relate and use percentages to describe their connection.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a line equation to solve problems with two related measurements and explain what the slope and intercept mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that straight lines often show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns between two sets of data, like clusters or trends.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child can study how two sets of data relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Data, Statistics, and Probability
description_plain: Your child learns about data, statistics, and probability.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can look at a graph and describe how two things change together, like when a line goes up or down, and draw a graph from a description.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a function to show a straight-line relationship between two things, find the rate of change and starting value, and explain what they mean.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child uses functions to show how different amounts are connected.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child understands that y = mx + b is a linear function with a straight-line graph and can give examples of functions that are not straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like with an equation, graph, table, or words.

subject: Math
description: Define a function as a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. Show that the graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output. (Function notation is not required for Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child understands a function is a rule that gives one output for each input and can show this with pairs of numbers on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions.
description_plain: Your child can define what functions are, find their values, and compare different functions.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically, and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection.
description_plain: Your child can solve two straight-line equations with two unknowns using algebra, estimate answers by graphing, and solve simple cases by looking.

subject: Math
description: Explain that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross because those points satisfy both equations.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve pairs of straight-line equations at the same time.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations with variables on both sides and whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with fractions and variables on both sides, including using the distributive property and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can give examples of straight-line equations with one solution, many solutions, or no solutions, and show which by simplifying the equation step-by-step.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step and multi-step linear equations with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to solve single and pairs of linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane; derive the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slope (steepness) is the same between any two points on a line and use the equations y = mx and y = mx + b to describe lines.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships and understand the unit rate as the slope, and compare different proportional relationships shown in various ways.

subject: Math
description: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how proportional relationships, lines, and straight-line equations are connected.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can do math with numbers in scientific notation, including mixing decimals and scientific notation, choose the right units for very big or small measurements, and understand scientific notation from technology.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and to express how many times as much one is than the other. For example, estimate the population of the United States as 3 times 108 and the population of the world as 7 times 109, and determine that the world population is more than 20 times larger.
description_plain: Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very big or small amounts and compare how many times bigger one is than another.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares (up to 100) and cube roots of small perfect cubes (up to 64). Know that √2 is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p and x³ = p, and can find the roots of small perfect squares and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use rules for exponents to rewrite and simplify expressions with whole number powers.

subject: Math
description: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child works with roots (like square roots) and whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g., π²).
description_plain: Your child can estimate and compare irrational numbers by using close rational numbers and place them approximately on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number. Define irrational numbers as numbers that are not rational.
description_plain: Your child understands that every number can be written as a decimal, knows that decimals for rational numbers eventually repeat, can change repeating decimals back into fractions, and knows that irrational numbers are numbers that cannot be written as fractions.

subject: Math
description: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child knows there are numbers that can't be written as fractions and can estimate them using fractions or decimals.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the structure of problems to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses tools in smart ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking and listen to others to understand math better.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and ideas in different ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child tries to understand problems and keeps working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes by folding them to see if both sides match.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines and by naming triangles as right, acute, or obtuse based on their angles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw points, lines, line segments, rays, and different types of angles, and find these parts in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and identify lines and angles and group shapes based on their lines and angles.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child can find the size of a whole angle by adding the sizes of its parts and solve problems to find missing angles using addition or subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles using a protractor and draw angles with a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child knows that an angle made by turning through n one-degree steps measures n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that an angle is measured by how much it turns around a point, with one degree being 1/360 of a full turn.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement:
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing a point and learns how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands what angles are and can measure them.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements in fractions and use the plot to solve addition and subtraction problems with fractions.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving simple fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems involving distances, time, volume, weight, and money using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division, including problems with fractions or decimals, and can show measurements on number lines.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two-column table.
description_plain: Your child understands how different units of measurement relate to each other (like kilometers to meters or hours to minutes) and can change measurements from bigger units to smaller ones and record these changes in a table.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve measuring things and changing measurements from larger units to smaller units.

subject: Math
description: Data, Statistics, and Probability
description_plain: Your child learns about data, statistics, and probability.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself.
description_plain: Your child can create number or shape patterns that follow a rule and notice new features in the pattern that weren’t obvious from the rule.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze patterns.
description_plain: Your child can create and study patterns in numbers or shapes.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1–100. Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1–100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1–100 is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can find all factor pairs of numbers up to 100, know that numbers are multiples of their factors, check if numbers up to 100 are multiples of one-digit numbers, and tell if numbers are prime or composite.

subject: Math
description: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child is getting to know how factors and multiples work.

subject: Math
description: Solve multistep word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, including problems with remainders, and write equations with letters for unknowns, checking if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that compare amounts by multiplying or dividing, using drawings and equations with symbols for unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35=5×7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5. Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication equations as comparisons, like knowing 35 = 5 × 7 means 35 is 5 times 7, and can write multiplication equations from words that compare amounts.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to hundredths by thinking about their size, use >, =, or < to show which is bigger, and explain why, making sure the decimals refer to the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators 10 or 100 as decimals.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100. (Students who can generate equivalent fractions can develop strategies for adding fractions with unlike denominators in general. But addition and subtraction with unlike denominators in general is not a requirement at this grade.)
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equal fraction with denominator 100 and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions as decimals and compare decimal fractions.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that multiply a fraction by a whole number using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a whole number means multiplying the numerator and can use models to show this.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands a fraction like a/b as a number made up of a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
