description: Describe the origins, structures, and functions of local government.
description_plain: Your child can describe where local government comes from, how it is set up, and what it does.

subject: Social Studies
description: State a position and reflect on possible objections to assumptions and implications of the position.
description_plain: Your child can share their opinion and think about reasons why others might disagree.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain a different perspective when exploring events or ideas.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain different points of view when learning about events or ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Regulate reactions to differing perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can control their feelings when hearing opinions that are different from their own.

subject: Social Studies
description: Appropriately express one's own emotions, thoughts, and values and identify how they influence behavior.
description_plain: Your child can express their feelings and thoughts in a good way and understand how these affect their actions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Restate the view or opinion of others with their reasoning when it is different from one's own.
description_plain: Your child can repeat someone else's opinion and reasons when they disagree with their own.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of the relationship between rights and responsibilities.
description_plain: Your child can give examples showing how rights and responsibilities are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify important personal rights in a democratic society and how they relate to others' rights.
description_plain: Your child can name important personal rights in a democracy and explain how they relate to others' rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and apply the elements of civil discourse. For example: Listening with respect, speaking in a respectful manner, and restating an opposing viewpoint or opinion.
description_plain: Your child can practice respectful talking and listening, including repeating others' opinions fairly.

subject: Social Studies
description: Respect the views and rights of others.
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of respecting other people's views and rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the perspectives of all parties participating in an exchange.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain what different people think during a trade or exchange.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize how members of a community rely on each other through exchanging goods and services, considering personal exchange behaviors.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in a community depend on each other by trading goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Cite evidence to show how trade benefits individuals, businesses, and communities, and increases interdependency.
description_plain: Your child can show how trade helps people, businesses, and communities and makes them depend on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize that different currencies exist and explain the functions of money. For example: Medium of exchange, store of value, and measure of value.
description_plain: Your child knows that different countries use different money and can explain what money is used for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the exchange of goods and services between businesses and consumers affects all parties.
description_plain: Your child can describe how buying and selling between businesses and customers affects everyone involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and give examples of forms of exchange. For example: Monetary exchange and barter.
description_plain: Your child can describe and give examples of ways people trade, like using money or swapping items.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the difference between producers and consumers and explain how they need each other.
description_plain: Your child can explain the difference between people who make things and people who buy things, and how they need each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how producers and consumers exchange goods and services in different ways.
description_plain: Your child understands how producers and consumers trade goods and services in different ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate a variety of places and communities and draw conclusions about regions.
description_plain: Your child can explore different places and communities and decide what makes regions unique.

subject: Social Studies
description: Characterize regions using different types of features such as physical, political, cultural, urban, and rural attributes.
description_plain: Your child can describe regions by their physical features, government, culture, and whether they are city or country areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of places that are similar and different from a local region.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of places that are like or different from their local area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the factors that make a region unique. For example: Cultural diversity, industry and agriculture, and landforms.
description_plain: Your child can identify what makes a region special, like its culture, jobs, or land shapes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Observe and describe the physical, cultural, and human-made characteristics of a local region. For example: The Eastern Plains, San Luis Valley, Pikes Peak, Northwest, Front Range, South Central, Southwest, and Western Slope.
description_plain: Your child can observe and describe the natural, cultural, and human-made parts of their local region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the concept of region through an examination of similarities and differences in places and communities.
description_plain: Your child understands what a region is by looking at how places and communities are alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Articulate the most effective geographic tools to access information needed for developing spatial thinking.
description_plain: Your child can choose the best map or tool to find information and think about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify geography-based problems and examine the ways that people have tried to solve them.
description_plain: Your child can find problems related to geography and learn how people have tried to fix them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the natural and man-made features of a specific area on a map.
description_plain: Your child can describe natural and human-made features shown on a map of a certain area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate oceans and continents, major countries, bodies of water, mountains, urban areas, the state of Colorado, and neighboring states on maps.
description_plain: Your child can find oceans, continents, major countries, mountains, cities, Colorado, and nearby states on maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Read and interpret information from geographic tools and formulate geographic questions.
description_plain: Your child can read maps and other geographic tools and ask questions about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic tools to develop spatial thinking skills.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and tools to help understand where things are and how places relate.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize how members of a community rely on each other and interact to influence the development of their communities.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in a community depend on and work with each other to help their community grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the history, interaction, and contribution of various peoples and cultures, including African American, Latino, Asian American, Hawaiian/Pacific Islander, Indigenous Peoples, LGBTQ, and religious minorities that have lived in or migrated to a community or region and how that migration has influenced change and development.
description_plain: Your child can talk about different groups of people who have lived in a community and explain how their arrival changed the area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of people, events, and developments that brought important changes to a community or region.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of people and events that brought important changes to a community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare past and present situations and events.
description_plain: Your child can compare what life was like in the past to life today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how people in the past influence the development and interaction of different communities or regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how people from the past helped shape communities and regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Ask questions to develop further understanding of reliability of various kinds of historical sources.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to learn how trustworthy different history sources are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Articulate the most effective kinds of historical sources to access information needed for understanding historic events.
description_plain: Your child can explain which types of history sources are best for learning about past events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare information from multiple sources recounting the same event.
description_plain: Your child can compare information from different sources that tell the same story.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of primary sources such as artifacts, pictures, oral histories, and documents, to help determine factual information about historical events.
description_plain: Your child can use things like old objects, pictures, stories, and documents to find facts about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare primary sources with works of fiction about the same topic.
description_plain: Your child can compare real history sources with fictional stories about the same topic.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare primary and secondary sources when explaining the past.
description_plain: Your child can explain the past by comparing original sources with later explanations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply economic reasoning skills to make informed personal financial decisions.
description_plain: Your child can use economic thinking to make smart choices about their own money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the origins, structures, and functions of governments to evaluate the impact on citizens and the global society.
description_plain: Your child can study how governments start, work, and affect people and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express an understanding of how civic participation affects policy by applying the rights and responsibilities of a citizen.
description_plain: Your child understands how being a citizen and taking part in the community can change laws and rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how scarce resources are allocated in societies through the analysis of individual choice, market interaction, and public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at how limited resources are shared through choices, markets, and government rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the characteristics of places and regions, and the changing nature among geographic and human interactions.
description_plain: Your child studies places and regions and how people and the environment change over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply geographic representations and perspectives to analyze human movement, spatial patterns, systems, and the connections and relationships among them.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and other tools to study how people move and connect in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical time periods and patterns of continuity and change, through multiple perspectives, within and among cultures and societies.
description_plain: Your child looks at history from many views to understand what stays the same and what changes in cultures and societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply the process of inquiry to examine and analyze how historical knowledge is viewed, constructed, and interpreted.
description_plain: Your child learns how to ask questions and think about how history is made and understood.

subject: Social Studies
description: Ask questions to develop further personal understanding of how to make informed purchases.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to better understand how to make smart buying choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Regulate one's emotions, thoughts, and behaviors in different situations when making a purchase.
description_plain: Your child can control their feelings and thoughts when deciding what to buy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Make connections between information gathered and personal experiences to apply and/or test solutions when making a purchase.
description_plain: Your child can connect what they learn with their own experiences to try out solutions when buying something.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze scenarios of choices including opportunity cost.
description_plain: Your child can think about different choices and what is given up when making those choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Determine the relationship between long-term goals and opportunity cost.
description_plain: Your child can explain how giving up something now can affect long-term goals.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define choice and opportunity cost.
description_plain: Your child understands what a choice is and what opportunity cost means.

subject: Social Studies
description: Determine the opportunity cost when making a choice.
description_plain: Your child can figure out what they give up when they make a choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize how members of a community rely on each other to make decisions and enact change.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in a community depend on each other to make decisions and create change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how a citizen might engage in local and state government to demonstrate their rights or initiate change.
description_plain: Your child can describe ways a citizen can take part in local and state government to show their rights or start change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the decisions of the state government affect local governments and interact with the federal government and sovereign indigenous nations.
description_plain: Your child can explain how state government decisions affect local governments and work with the federal government and indigenous nations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the services the state government provides and how those services are funded.
description_plain: Your child can name services the state government provides and explain how they pay for them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain a variety of roles leaders, citizens, and others play in state government.
description_plain: Your child can identify different roles that leaders, citizens, and others have in state government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the historical foundation and events that led to the Colorado Constitution and the formation of the three branches of Colorado government.
description_plain: Your child can explain the history that led to Colorado’s Constitution and how its government branches were formed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origins, structures, and functions of the Colorado government.
description_plain: Your child understands where Colorado’s government came from, how it is set up, and what it does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in social or community activities.
description_plain: Your child participates in activities that help their community or social groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives when exploring issues faced by the state of Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can see and explain different points of view about problems in Colorado.

subject: Social Studies
description: Regulate reactions to differing perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can manage their reactions when they hear opinions that are different from their own.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and use appropriate sources to investigate and analyze issues from multiple diverse perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can find and use good sources to look at issues from many different viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss how various individuals and groups influence the way an issue affecting the state is viewed and resolved. Including but not limited to the contributions of African Americans, Latinos, Japanese Americans, Indigenous Peoples, LGBTQ, and religious groups.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how different people and groups, including African Americans, Latinos, Japanese Americans, Indigenous Peoples, LGBTQ, and religious groups, affect how problems in Colorado are seen and solved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Provide supportive arguments for both sides of a current public policy debate involving diverse stakeholders.
description_plain: Your child can give reasons for both sides of a public debate that involves many different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of issues faced by the state of Colorado and develop possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of problems Colorado faces and think of possible solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate multiple perspectives on civic issues.
description_plain: Your child investigates different viewpoints on community and government issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Set goals and develop strategies which take into account positive and negative incentives in order to remain focused on learning goals.
description_plain: Your child sets goals and plans ways to stay focused by thinking about rewards and consequences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how productive resources (natural, human, and capital) have influenced the types of goods produced and services provided in Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural resources, people’s work, and tools have affected what goods and services are made in Colorado.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of the kinds of goods and services produced in Colorado, in different historical periods, and their connection to economic incentives.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of goods and services made in Colorado during different times and how rewards influenced them.

subject: Social Studies
description: In a given situation, create a plan of appropriate incentives to achieve a desired result. For example: Offering a prize to the person who picks up the most trash on the playground.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan using rewards or consequences to reach a goal, like offering a prize for picking up trash.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define positive and negative economic incentives and describe how people typically respond to those incentives.
description_plain: Your child understands what positive and negative incentives are and how people usually react to them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people respond to positive and negative incentives.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people respond to rewards and consequences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the problems faced by people in Colorado because of the physical environment they encountered.
description_plain: Your child can describe problems people in Colorado face because of the land and environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how places in Colorado are connected by movement of goods, services, and technology.
description_plain: Your child can explain how places in Colorado are connected by moving goods, services, and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how people use geographic factors in creating settlements and have adapted to and modified the local physical environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people choose where to live based on the land and how they change the environment to fit their needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how physical environments influence immigration into the state.
description_plain: Your child understands how the land and environment affect why people move into Colorado.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the physical environment provides opportunities for and places constraints on human activities.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the land offers chances for activities but also limits what people can do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the relationship between the physical environment and its effect on human activity.
description_plain: Your child will explore how the land and environment affect what people do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Ask questions to develop further understanding about the geography and development of Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to learn more about Colorado's geography and how it grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Articulate the most effective tools to access information about the geography of Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can explain the best tools to find information about Colorado's geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe similarities and differences between the physical geography of Colorado and its neighboring states.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Colorado's land is like or different from nearby states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Illustrate, using geographic tools, how places in Colorado have changed and developed over time due to human activity.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and tools to show how places in Colorado have changed because of people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and investigate geographic questions about Colorado in relation to other places.
description_plain: Your child can make and explore questions about Colorado's geography compared to other places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic grids, including latitude and longitude, to locate places and answer questions about maps and images of Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can use latitude and longitude to find places on maps of Colorado and answer questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Answer questions about Colorado regions using maps and other geographic tools.
description_plain: Your child can answer questions about different parts of Colorado using maps and other tools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic tools to research and answer questions about Colorado geography.
description_plain: Your child will use maps and tools to find answers about Colorado's geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and describe cause-and-effect relationships within Colorado history and draw conclusions about how various events and people affected the development of the state.
description_plain: Your child can explain how events and people caused changes in Colorado's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of various technological developments. For example: Changes in mining technologies, agricultural technology (center pivot irrigation), transportation, early 20th century industrial developments, and 20th century nuclear and computer technologies.
description_plain: Your child can describe how new technologies, like mining tools and computers, changed Colorado.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe both past and present interactions among the people and cultures in Colorado. For example: African Americans, Latinos, Asian Americans, Indigenous Peoples, LGBTQ, and religious groups.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how different groups of people have lived and worked together in Colorado, both long ago and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship between major events in Colorado history and events in United States history during the same era. Including but not limited to: Colorado statehood, the Ludlow and Sand Creek Massacres, creation of national parks in Colorado, the Great Depression, the Dust Bowl, Amaché, Chicano movement, and busing in Denver.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important events in Colorado happened at the same time as events in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct a timeline of the major events in Colorado history.
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline showing the main events in Colorado's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the historical eras, individuals, groups, ideas, and themes in Colorado history and their relationship to key events in the United States within the same historical period.
description_plain: Your child will learn about important times, people, and ideas in Colorado's history and how they connect to U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and describe cause-and-effect relationships in the history of Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can explain how one event in Colorado's history led to another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the perspectives of the various groups important in Colorado history when exploring the development of the state. For example: African American, Latino, Asian American, Indigenous Peoples, LGBTQ, religious groups, working class, and labor unions.
description_plain: Your child can identify different groups in Colorado's history and explain their views about how the state grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the multiple perspectives of settler colonialism/Westward Expansion and the impact on the political and cultural landscape of the region presently known as Colorado.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how settlers moving west affected Colorado's politics and culture from different points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe how political and cultural groups have affected the development of the region. Including but not limited to: African American, Latino, Asian American, Indigenous Peoples, religious groups, and European settlers.
description_plain: Your child can describe how different groups, like Native Americans and settlers, helped shape Colorado.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain, through multiple perspectives, the human interactions among people and cultures that are indigenous to or migrated to present-day Colorado. Including but not limited to: historic tribes of Colorado, the Ute Mountain Ute, Southern Ute, Spanish explorers, trappers, and traders.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people who lived in or moved to Colorado interacted, including Native tribes and explorers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify cause-and-effect relationships using primary sources to understand the history of Colorado's development.
description_plain: Your child can use old documents to find cause-and-effect relationships in Colorado's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Draw inferences about Colorado history from primary sources such as journals, diaries, maps, treaties, oral histories, etc.
description_plain: Your child can make guesses about Colorado's history by looking at journals, maps, and other old sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze primary and secondary sources from multiple points of view to develop an understanding of the history of Colorado.
description_plain: Your child will study different sources to understand Colorado's history from many viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply economic reasoning skills to make informed personal financial decisions.
description_plain: Your child can use economic thinking to make smart choices about their own money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the origins, structures, and functions of governments to evaluate the impact on citizens and the global society.
description_plain: Your child can study how governments start, work, and affect people and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express an understanding of how civic participation affects policy by applying the rights and responsibilities of a citizen.
description_plain: Your child understands how being a citizen and taking part in the community can change laws and rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how scarce resources are allocated in societies through the analysis of individual choice, market interaction, and public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at how limited resources are shared through choices, markets, and government rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the characteristics of places and regions, and the changing nature among geographic and human interactions.
description_plain: Your child studies places and regions and how people and the environment change over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply geographic representations and perspectives to analyze human movement, spatial patterns, systems, and the connections and relationships among them.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and other tools to study how people move and connect in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical time periods and patterns of continuity and change, through multiple perspectives, within and among cultures and societies.
description_plain: Your child looks at history from many views to understand what stays the same and what changes in cultures and societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply the process of inquiry to examine and analyze how historical knowledge is viewed, constructed, and interpreted.
description_plain: Your child learns how to ask questions and think about how history is made and understood.

subject: Social Studies
description: Ask questions to develop further personal understanding when choosing financial institutions and financial products.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to better understand how to choose banks or financial products.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express one's own emotions, thoughts, and values and identify how they influence behavior when making decisions regarding choosing a financial institution or financial product.
description_plain: Your child can share their feelings and thoughts and see how these affect their money choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Make connections between information gathered and personal experiences to apply and/or test solutions when choosing a financial institution or financial product.
description_plain: Your child can connect what they learn with their own experiences to make smart money decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a way to keep track of money spent and money saved.
description_plain: Your child can create a way to keep track of money they spend and save.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between different types of financial institutions such as banks and credit unions, and the services provided. For example: Checking accounts, savings accounts, investments, and loans.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between banks and credit unions and what services they offer, like checking or savings accounts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Establish the function of banking.
description_plain: Your child understands what banks do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between saving and investing.
description_plain: Your child can explain the difference between saving money and investing money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how individuals use financial institutions to manage personal finances.
description_plain: Your child will learn how people use banks and other financial places to manage their money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives when exploring the events leading to the creation of the United States government and the principles of American democracy.
description_plain: Your child can explain different viewpoints about how the U.S. government was created and the ideas behind American democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the Constitution balances the power of national and state governments.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Constitution shares power between the national and state governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the origins, structures, and functions of the three branches of the United States government and the relationships among them.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the three branches of the U.S. government do and how they work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify political principles of American democracy and how the Constitution and Bill of Rights reflect and preserve these principles.
description_plain: Your child can identify important American democratic ideas and how the Constitution and Bill of Rights protect them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the foundational documents and significance of the events that led to the establishment of the United States government. Including but not limited to the Philadelphia Convention, the Declaration of Independence, the Articles of Confederation, the Iroquois Confederacy, the Constitution, and the Bill of Rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain important documents and events that helped start the U.S. government, like the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the origins, structures, and functions of the United States government.
description_plain: Your child will explore how the U.S. government began, how it is set up, and how it works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Connect knowledge of the foundations of citizenship in the United States to personal ideas/understandings.
description_plain: Your child can connect what they learn about U.S. citizenship to their own ideas and understanding.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize how members of a community rely on each other through a variety of ways when creating rules and norms.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in a community depend on each other to make rules and agree on how to behave.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss how the concept of citizenship changed over time in the early American colonies.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how the idea of citizenship changed in the early American colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of group and individual actions that illustrate civic ideals in the founding of the United States. For example: Freedom, rule of law, equality, civility, cooperation, respect, responsibility, and civic participation.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of actions by groups and individuals that show important American values like freedom, respect, and responsibility.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and explain examples of individual rights as a foundation of citizenship.
description_plain: Your child understands and can explain examples of individual rights that are important for being a citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct an understanding of the foundations, rights, and responsibilities of citizenship in the United States.
description_plain: Your child learns about the basics of citizenship, including rights and responsibilities, in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize how members of a community rely on each other through trade and exchange.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in a community depend on each other through trading goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate to form hypotheses, make observations, and draw conclusions about the development of the systems of exchange in the United States.
description_plain: Your child investigates and makes guesses, observes, and draws conclusions about how trading systems developed in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain some of the challenges that American colonists faced that would eventually lead them to the creation of commercial banks.
description_plain: Your child can explain some problems American colonists faced that led to the creation of banks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how patterns of trade evolved within Early America.
description_plain: Your child describes how trade changed and grew in early America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the goods and services that were traded among different cultures and regions.
description_plain: Your child identifies goods and services that different cultures and regions traded with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare ways in which people and communities exchanged goods and services. For example: Barter and monetary exchange.
description_plain: Your child compares different ways people and communities exchanged goods and services, like trading items or using money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of the productive resources and explain how they are used to produce goods and services. For example: Land, labor, and capital.
description_plain: Your child can name resources like land, work, and money and explain how they help make goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how patterns of trade shaped the development of Early America.
description_plain: Your child explains how trade patterns helped shape early America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives when exploring ideas about conflict in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain different points of view when learning about conflicts in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Consider purpose, formality of context and audience, and distinct cultural norms when planning the content, mode, delivery, and expression of analysis of historical events and movements.
description_plain: Your child thinks about who they are sharing information with and how formal or informal it should be when talking about historical events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of the influence of geography on the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child gives examples of how geography affected U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how cooperation and conflict among diverse groups of people contribute(d) to political, economic, and social divisions in the United States.
description_plain: Your child analyzes how working together and conflicts between different groups caused political, economic, and social divisions in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and analyze how specific physical and political features influenced historical events, movements, and adaptation to the environment.
description_plain: Your child describes how land features and political boundaries influenced historical events and how people adapted to their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain migration, trade, and cultural patterns that result from interactions among people, groups, and cultures.
description_plain: Your child explains how migration, trade, and culture changed because of interactions between people and groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify variables associated with discovery, exploration, and migration.
description_plain: Your child identifies reasons and factors related to discovery, exploration, and migration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine causes and consequences of movement.
description_plain: Your child examines why people moved and what happened because of that movement.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate geographic resources to form hypotheses, make observations, and draw conclusions about communities in the United States.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and other geographic tools to make guesses, observe, and learn about communities in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the influence of accessible resources on the development of local and regional communities throughout North America and the United States.
description_plain: Your child describes how available resources affected the growth of local and regional communities in North America and the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic tools to identify, locate, and describe places and regions in North America and the United States and suggest reasons for their location.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and tools to find and describe places and regions in North America and the U.S. and explains why they are located there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Answer questions about regions of North America and the United States using various types of maps.
description_plain: Your child answers questions about regions in North America and the U.S. using different kinds of maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic tools and sources to research and answer questions about United States geography.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and other geographic sources to research and answer questions about U.S. geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives including African Americans, Indigenous Peoples, Latinos, Asian Americans, Hawaiian/Pacific Islanders, LGBTQ, and religious groups when exploring events, ideas, issues in United States history.
description_plain: Your child identifies and explains different perspectives from groups like African Americans, Indigenous Peoples, Latinos, Asian Americans, Pacific Islanders, LGBTQ, and religious groups when learning about U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate causes and effects of significant events in early United States history. For example: The establishment of Jamestown, George Washington's crossing of the Delaware River, the French and Indian War, and the Constitutional Convention.
description_plain: Your child investigates causes and effects of important early U.S. events like Jamestown’s founding, Washington crossing the Delaware, the French and Indian War, and the Constitutional Convention.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze important political, social, economic, and military developments leading to and during the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child analyzes key political, social, economic, and military events leading up to and during the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the political, social, and economic reasons for the settlement of the European and American colonies and how it affected Indigenous Peoples and enslaved Africans.
description_plain: Your child describes why European and American colonies were settled and how this affected Indigenous Peoples and enslaved Africans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the contributions of significant individuals and groups of Indigenous Peoples, enslaved individuals, and European colonists through the American Revolution. For example: Crispus Attucks, Sybil Luddington, Benjamin Banneker, Thomas Jefferson, and Patrick Henry.
description_plain: Your child identifies and describes important contributions by Indigenous Peoples, enslaved individuals, and European colonists during the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain interactions among various groups such as Indigenous Peoples, enslaved individuals (both Indigenous and African), and European colonists. For example: The cultural genocide of Indigenous Peoples, chattel slavery of Africans, the League of the Iroquois, Spanish missions, and trade networks.
description_plain: Your child explains how different groups like Indigenous Peoples, enslaved people, and European colonists interacted, including examples like slavery, trade, and alliances.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the historical eras, individuals, groups, ideas, and themes in North America from early Indigenous Peoples through the European Age of Exploration/post-Columbian colonization and the establishment of the United States Government.
description_plain: Your child studies important historical times, people, groups, ideas, and themes from early Indigenous Peoples through European exploration and the start of the U.S. government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives when exploring events, ideas, and issues in United States history.
description_plain: Your child identifies and explains different viewpoints when learning about events and ideas in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Make observations and draw conclusions from a variety of sources when studying American history.
description_plain: Your child looks at many sources to make observations and draw conclusions about American history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze cartoons, artifacts, artwork, charts, and graphs related to eras and themes in early North America.
description_plain: Your child analyzes cartoons, artifacts, artwork, charts, and graphs related to early North American history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret timelines of eras and themes in North America from early Indigenous Peoples through the European Age of Exploration/post-Columbian colonization and the establishment of the United States Government.
description_plain: Your child reads and understands timelines about important times and themes from early Indigenous Peoples through European exploration and the start of the U.S. government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine significant historical documents. For example: The Proclamation of 1763, the Stamp Act, the Declaration of Independence, the Constitution, and treaties with Indigenous Nations such as the Two Row Wampum Treaty.
description_plain: Your child studies important historical documents like the Proclamation of 1763, the Stamp Act, the Declaration of Independence, the Constitution, and treaties with Indigenous Nations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize how historical context can affect the perspective of historical sources.
description_plain: Your child understands how the time and place when a source was created can affect its point of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze primary and secondary sources from multiple points of view to develop an understanding of early United States history.
description_plain: Your child can look at different kinds of historical documents and stories to understand early United States history from many viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply economic reasoning skills to make informed personal financial decisions.
description_plain: Your child can use economic thinking to make smart choices about their own money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the origins, structures, and functions of governments to evaluate the impact on citizens and the global society.
description_plain: Your child can study how governments start, work, and affect people and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express an understanding of how civic participation affects policy by applying the rights and responsibilities of a citizen.
description_plain: Your child understands how being a citizen and taking part in the community can change laws and rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how scarce resources are allocated in societies through the analysis of individual choice, market interaction, and public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at how limited resources are shared through choices, markets, and government rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the characteristics of places and regions, and the changing nature among geographic and human interactions.
description_plain: Your child studies places and regions and how people and the environment change over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply geographic representations and perspectives to analyze human movement, spatial patterns, systems, and the connections and relationships among them.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and other tools to study how people move and connect in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical time periods and patterns of continuity and change, through multiple perspectives, within and among cultures and societies.
description_plain: Your child looks at history from many views to understand what stays the same and what changes in cultures and societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply the process of inquiry to examine and analyze how historical knowledge is viewed, constructed, and interpreted.
description_plain: Your child learns how to ask questions and think about how history is made and understood.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess personal strengths and limitations with a well-grounded sense of confidence, optimism, and a growth mindset.
description_plain: Your child understands their own strengths and weaknesses and stays confident and positive while learning and growing.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives (cultural and global) when exploring economic events, ideas, and issues within the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and explain different cultural and global views when learning about economic events in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of cause and effect related to personal financial decisions.
description_plain: Your child understands how their personal money choices can cause different results.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand how basic budgeting, investing, saving, and personal behavior with money affects the economic system as a consumer and/or producer.
description_plain: Your child understands how budgeting, saving, investing, and how they use money affect the economy as a buyer or seller.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how external factors might influence spending decisions for different individuals.
description_plain: Your child can think about how outside factors might change how different people decide to spend money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explore how consumer spending decisions and demand impact market economies.
description_plain: Your child explores how what people buy and want affects how markets work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the roles of buyers and sellers in product, labor, and financial markets.
description_plain: Your child can explain what buyers and sellers do in markets for products, jobs, and money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the role of consumers and businesses within the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child investigates how consumers and businesses work together in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply ethical perspectives/concepts to an ethical question/situation/scenario.
description_plain: Your child can use ideas about right and wrong to think about and solve ethical questions or situations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Look for and find value in different perspectives expressed by others in the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child looks for and appreciates different opinions from people in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan and evaluate complex solutions to global challenges, in the Western Hemisphere, which are appropriate to their contexts, using multiple disciplinary perspectives such as ethnic, historical, and scientific.
description_plain: Your child plans and checks solutions to big problems in the Western Hemisphere using ideas from history, culture, and science.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how individuals interpret messages differently, how values and points of view are included or excluded, and how media can influence beliefs and behaviors.
description_plain: Your child learns how people understand messages differently, how some views are included or left out, and how media can change beliefs and actions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the historical, social, and cultural influences of minorities on civil governments of the United States, and the rest of the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child investigates how minority groups have influenced government in the United States and the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the value each nation and/or culture places on economic prosperity, individual liberty, human rights, freedom of the press, freedom of religion, equal rights, and respect for neighbors.
description_plain: Your child explains what different countries and cultures value, like freedom, human rights, and respect for others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the economic components of the different systems of government in the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child compares how different governments in the Western Hemisphere handle their economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how different systems of government relate to their citizens in the Western Hemisphere and how systems of government create advantages for some of their citizens and disadvantages for others.
description_plain: Your child identifies how different governments relate to their people and how some groups may benefit or be treated unfairly.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe structures and functions of different systems of government in the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child describes how different governments in the Western Hemisphere are set up and how they work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine and explain the development of foundational principles of government systems in the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child examines and explains how important government ideas started in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the development and features of systems of government in the Western Hemisphere relate to their citizens.
description_plain: Your child explains how governments in the Western Hemisphere affect the lives of their citizens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in social or community activities.
description_plain: Your child takes part in social or community activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Follow a process identified by others to help generate ideas, negotiate roles and responsibilities, and respect consensus in decision making.
description_plain: Your child follows steps to share ideas, agree on roles, and respect group decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Look for and find value in different perspectives expressed by others.
description_plain: Your child looks for and values different opinions from others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan and evaluate complex solutions to global challenges in the Western Hemisphere, using multiple disciplinary lenses such as ethnic, historical, and scientific.
description_plain: Your child plans and checks solutions to big problems in the Western Hemisphere using ideas from history, culture, and science.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how a specific problem can manifest itself at local, regional, and global levels, and how media can influence beliefs and behaviors.
description_plain: Your child thinks about how a problem can happen locally, regionally, and globally, and how media can affect what people believe and do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how individuals in the Western Hemisphere interpret messages differently, and how values and points of view are included or excluded.
description_plain: Your child studies how people in the Western Hemisphere understand messages differently and how some views are included or left out.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical examples illustrating how people from diverse backgrounds such as African American, Latino, Asian American, and Indigenous Peoples in the Western Hemisphere perceived, reacted to, and influenced national and international policies and issues.
description_plain: Your child identifies examples of how people from different backgrounds in the Western Hemisphere influenced national and international policies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze political issues from national and global perspectives over time in North America, South America, and the Caribbean.
description_plain: Your child analyzes political issues in North America, South America, and the Caribbean from national and global views over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political ideas of significant people and groups interact, are interconnected, and influence nations and regions in the Western Hemisphere, both in the past and today.
description_plain: Your child explains how important political ideas and groups connect and affect countries in the Western Hemisphere, past and present.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine changes and connections in ideas about citizenship in different times and places in the Western Hemisphere. For example: Indigenous Peoples are either denied citizenship or citizenship is not always desired; immigration and a nation's quota preferences; and the changes in naturalization requirements change over time.
description_plain: Your child examines how ideas about citizenship have changed and connected across different times and places in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine civic participation within different governmental systems of the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child studies how people participate in government in different countries of the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Make connections between information gathered and personal experiences to research economic questions.
description_plain: Your child connects what they learn with their own experiences to research economic questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Look for and find value in different economic perspectives expressed by others (Adaptability and Flexibility).
description_plain: Your child looks for and appreciates different economic opinions from others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan and evaluate complex solutions to global economic system challenges using multiple disciplinary lenses such as cultural, historical, and scientific (Civic Engagement, Global and Cultural Awareness).
description_plain: Your child plans and checks solutions to global economic problems using ideas from culture, history, and science.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of competition and supply and demand in the determination of prices and wages in a market economy.
description_plain: Your child describes how competition and supply and demand help decide prices and wages in a market.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how different societies view and use money and resources.
description_plain: Your child explains how different societies use money and resources in their own ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use economic reasoning to explain how specialization of production in a country can result in more interdependence. For example: International trade patterns.
description_plain: Your child uses economic thinking to explain how countries depend on each other through trade and specialized production.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how current economic systems in the Western Hemisphere (such as traditional, command, market, and mixed) developed.
description_plain: Your child describes how economic systems like traditional, command, market, and mixed economies developed in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate how different economic systems developed based on access to resources, societal values, and human experiences, in order to address the problem of scarcity.
description_plain: Your child investigates how different economic systems formed to solve the problem of limited resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply knowledge to set goals, make informed decisions and transfer to new contexts.
description_plain: Your child uses what they know to set goals, make smart choices, and apply learning to new situations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan and evaluate complex solutions to global challenges within the Western Hemisphere that are appropriate to their contexts using multiple disciplinary lenses such as ethnic, historical, and scientific.
description_plain: Your child can create and review detailed plans to solve big problems in the Western Hemisphere by looking at them from different viewpoints like culture, history, and science.

subject: Social Studies
description: Look for and find value in studying different perspectives expressed by others.
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of learning from different people's viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use characteristics to define physical and political regions of the past and present.
description_plain: Your child can describe physical and political areas from the past and today by their features.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze positive and negative interactions of human and physical systems in the Western Hemisphere and give examples of how people have adapted to and modified their physical environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and nature affect each other in the Western Hemisphere and give examples of how people have changed or adapted to their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify physical features of the Western Hemisphere and explain their effects on people who reside in those regions.
description_plain: Your child can identify natural features in the Western Hemisphere and explain how they influence the people living there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify and analyze the types of human and geographic connections between places and regions.
description_plain: Your child can group and study how people and places are connected in different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how regional differences and perspectives in the Western Hemisphere impact human and environmental interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands how differences between regions in the Western Hemisphere affect how people and the environment interact.

subject: Social Studies
description: Revisit, reflect on, and revise inquiry questions based on analysis of geographic data.
description_plain: Your child can review and change their questions based on studying geographic information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply and communicate solutions by formulating an action plan for real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can create and share a plan to solve real-world problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate task management attributes associated with producing high quality products. For example: (a) Work positively and ethically; (b) Manage time and projects effectively; (c) Multi-task, and; (d) Clearly communicate with others.
description_plain: Your child works well with others, manages time and tasks, handles several jobs at once, and communicates clearly to produce quality work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Collaborate with others to plan and evaluate complex solutions to global challenges within the Western Hemisphere using multiple disciplinary lenses such as ethnic, historical, and scientific.
description_plain: Your child works with others to plan and review detailed solutions to big problems in the Western Hemisphere using different viewpoints like culture, history, and science.

subject: Social Studies
description: Engage in novel approaches, directions, ideas, and/or perspectives while using the inquiry process to analyze primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child tries new ideas and ways of thinking when studying original and other sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply knowledge to set goals, make informed decisions and transfer to new contexts.
description_plain: Your child uses what they know to set goals, make smart choices, and apply learning to new situations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic tools and sources to research and make geographic inferences and predictions about the Western Hemisphere.

description: Look for and find value in different perspectives expressed by others.
description_plain: Your child understands the value of hearing and learning from different viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine different historical perspectives expressed in primary and secondary sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan and evaluate complex solutions to global challenges within the Western Hemisphere using multiple disciplinary lenses such as ethnic, historical, and scientific.
description_plain: Your child plans and reviews detailed solutions to big problems in the Western Hemisphere by looking at them from different views like culture, history, and science.

subject: Social Studies

description: Make connections between information gathered and personal experiences to create, research, and revise historical questions.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of the social, political, cultural, and economic development in the Western Hemisphere. For example: The extension of networks of communication, colonial empires, patterns of migration over time, and international trade.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how societies in the Western Hemisphere have grown socially, politically, culturally, and economically, like through trade and migration.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child understands the history behind important people, events, and ideas, including different viewpoints, like those during European colonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people, cultures, and ideas interact and are interconnected in the Western Hemisphere and how they have impacted modern times. For example: The "Great Dying" of Indigenous Peoples in the Americas and its consequences; rapid deforestation of the Amazon; anti-colonial and nationalist movements, the Columbian Exchange, and revolutions in energy.
description_plain: Your child explains how people, cultures, and ideas in the Western Hemisphere have connected and influenced modern times, such as through trade and movements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the historical eras, individuals, groups, ideas, and themes within regions of the Western Hemisphere and their relationships with one another.
description_plain: Your child investigates important times, people, groups, ideas, and themes in the Western Hemisphere and how they relate to each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize primary and secondary sources to examine how individuals interpret messages differently, how values and points of view are included or excluded, and how media can influence beliefs and behaviors.
description_plain: Your child uses original and other sources to see how people understand messages differently and how media can shape beliefs and actions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Respect different perspectives expressed by others.
description_plain: Your child shows respect for different viewpoints shared by others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Engage in novel approaches, moves, directions, ideas, and/or perspectives while using inquiry and primary sources.
description_plain: Your child tries new ideas and ways of thinking while studying original sources and asking questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Make connections between information gathered and personal experiences to research historical questions.
description_plain: Your child connects what they learn with their own experiences to research historical questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Gather, organize, synthesize, and critique information, from multiple and diverse perspectives, to determine if it is sufficient to answer historical questions about the Western Hemisphere. For example: Indigenous People, Latinos, African Americans, Asian Americans, Hawaiian/Pacific Islander, LGBTQ, and religious minorities and differing opinions within such groups.
description_plain: Your child gathers and reviews information from many viewpoints to decide if it answers historical questions about groups like Indigenous Peoples and others in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple primary and secondary sources while formulating historical questions about the Western Hemisphere. For example: Oral histories, art, artifacts, eyewitness accounts, letters, and diaries, real or simulated historical sites, charts, graphs, diagrams, and written texts.
description_plain: Your child studies many original and other sources while asking historical questions about the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways different cultures record history in the Western Hemisphere through written and oral sources.
description_plain: Your child learns how different cultures in the Western Hemisphere record history through writing and storytelling.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze and interpret primary and secondary sources to ask and research historical questions about the Western Hemisphere (including North America, South America, Central America, and the islands of the Caribbean).
description_plain: Your child studies original and other sources to ask and research historical questions about the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply economic reasoning skills to make informed personal financial decisions.
description_plain: Your child can use economic thinking to make smart choices about their own money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the origins, structures, and functions of governments to evaluate the impact on citizens and the global society.
description_plain: Your child can study how governments start, work, and affect people and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express an understanding of how civic participation affects policy by applying the rights and responsibilities of a citizen.
description_plain: Your child understands how being a citizen and taking part in the community can change laws and rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how scarce resources are allocated in societies through the analysis of individual choice, market interaction, and public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at how limited resources are shared through choices, markets, and government rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the characteristics of places and regions, and the changing nature among geographic and human interactions.
description_plain: Your child studies places and regions and how people and the environment change over time.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child uses maps and other tools to study how people move and connect in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical time periods and patterns of continuity and change, through multiple perspectives, within and among cultures and societies.
description_plain: Your child looks at history from many views to understand what stays the same and what changes in cultures and societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply the process of inquiry to examine and analyze how historical knowledge is viewed, constructed, and interpreted.
description_plain: Your child learns how to ask questions and think about how history is made and understood.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain multiple perspectives (cultural and global) when exploring events, ideas, issues.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain different cultural and global viewpoints when learning about events and ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of cause and effect related to personal decisions.
description_plain: Your child understands how their personal choices can cause different results.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast goods and services available to consumers within different cultures as they developed in the Eastern Hemisphere throughout history.
description_plain: Your child compares goods and services from different cultures in the Eastern Hemisphere throughout history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how the distribution of resources impacts consumerism.
description_plain: Your child summarizes how the way resources are shared affects what people buy and use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define resources from an economic and personal finance perspective.
description_plain: Your child can explain what resources are from both money and personal use points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the role of consumers and businesses within the Eastern Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child will explore how buyers and sellers work together in countries of the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Look for and find value in different perspectives expressed by others.
description_plain: Your child understands how to recognize and appreciate different opinions from others.

description_plain: Your child can create and review detailed plans to solve problems in the Eastern Hemisphere by looking at culture, history, science, and ethnicity.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how a specific problem can manifest itself at local, regional, and global levels, and how media can influence beliefs and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply ethical perspectives/concepts to an ethical question/situation/scenario.

subject: Social Studies

For example: The protection of human rights; the role of non-governmental organizations (NGOs), the Red Cross, World Health Organization, and United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organization (UNESCO).

description: Follow a process identified by others to help generate ideas, negotiate roles and responsibilities, and respect consensus in decision making.

description: Investigate similarities and differences of civic participation within different governmental systems of the Eastern Hemisphere.
